title: Arrays and Loops in C
description: How to declare a fixed-size array, read and change its elements by index, and use a for loop to visit every element.
congratsText: You mastered Arrays and Loops in C!

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
Objectives
  • Declare and fill a fixed-size array with starting values
  • Read and change array elements using an index
  • Use a for loop to print or add up every element in an array

sections.title: What Is an Array?
sections.lesson:
What Is an Array?
A variable holds one value. An array holds many values of the same type, stored one after another under a single name.
Think of an array as a row of numbered boxes. Each box holds one value, and each box has a position number called an index.
The first box is always index 0, not index 1.

quiz.question: What does an array store, compared to a normal variable?
options: Many values of the same type, in one named row of boxes
One value that can change type at any time
A single value, exactly like a normal variable
A list of variable names, not their values

sections.title: Declaring and Initializing an Array
sections.lesson:
Declaring and Initializing an Array
An array declaration lists the type, a name, and how many boxes to make. The size is fixed the moment the array is created.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88};
This makes 5 int boxes named scores, filled left to right with the values in the braces. scores[0] is 90, scores[4] is 88.

quiz.question: In `int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88};`, what does the 5 control?
options: How many boxes the array has
The value stored in the first box
How many times the array can be resized
The highest value allowed in the array

sections.title: Reading Elements by Index
sections.lesson:
Reading Elements by Index
To read one value out of an array, write the array name followed by an index in square brackets.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; printf("%d", scores[0]); // 90 printf("%d", scores[3]); // 92
scores[0] is the first box, scores[3] is the fourth box. Indexing always starts at 0, so the last valid index is one less than the array's size.

quiz.question: Given int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88};, what does scores[3] print?

sections.title: Writing to an Element
sections.lesson:
Writing to an Element
An array element can be changed the same way any variable can — assign a new value to that index.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; scores[0] = 100; printf("%d", scores[0]); // 100
Only the box at index 0 changes. The rest of the array stays exactly as it was.

quiz.question: After scores[0] = 100; runs, what happens to scores[1]?
options: scores[1] stays exactly the same
scores[1] also becomes 100
scores[1] is deleted from the array
scores[1] shifts into scores[0]'s old spot

sections.title: The for Loop
sections.lesson:
The for Loop
A for loop repeats a block of code a set number of times. It has three parts: a starting value, a condition that keeps it going, and a step that runs after each repeat.
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("%d ", i); }
i starts at 0, the loop keeps running while i < 5, and i++ adds 1 to i after each repeat. This prints 0 1 2 3 4.

quiz.question: In for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++), when does the loop stop repeating?
options: As soon as i < 5 is no longer true
After exactly 5 seconds have passed
As soon as i reaches 0
It never stops on its own

sections.title: Looping to Print an Array
sections.lesson:
Looping to Print an Array
A for loop's counter can be used as an array index. As i counts from 0 to 4, scores[i] visits every box in order.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("%d ", scores[i]); }
This prints all 5 values without writing 5 separate printf lines. i < 5 matches the array's size, so the loop never asks for a box that doesn't exist.

quiz.question: Why does the loop use i < 5 for an array declared as scores[5]?
options: So the loop visits index 0 through 4, matching the array's 5 boxes
So the loop visits index 1 through 5, matching the array's 5 boxes
Because 5 is the value stored in scores[0]
Because for loops always stop at 5 by default

sections.title: Looping to Sum an Array
sections.lesson:
Looping to Sum an Array
A loop can also build up a total. A variable started at 0 collects each element's value as the loop runs.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; int total = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { total = total + scores[i]; } printf("%d", total); // 433
total starts at 0 so the first value added isn't thrown off by leftover data. Each repeat adds one more box's value onto total.

quiz.question: In the sum loop above, what is total right before the loop starts?
options: The value of scores[4]

sections.title: Common Array Mistakes
sections.lesson:
Common Array Mistakes
The most common array bug is asking for a box that doesn't exist. An array of size 5 has valid indexes 0 through 4 — index 5 is one step past the end.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; printf("%d", scores[5]); // reads memory outside the array
C does not stop this at compile time. The program keeps running and prints whatever value happens to sit in that memory spot — not a real array value.
The same bug hides in a loop that uses <= instead of <: a loop condition of i <= 5 also reads scores[5] on its last repeat.

quiz.question: Why is scores[5] a problem for an array declared as int scores[5]?
options: Valid indexes only go from 0 to 4, so 5 is outside the array
5 is reserved as the array's total-size value
C automatically resizes the array to fit index 5
scores[5] always causes an immediate compiler error

sections.title: Recap
sections.lesson:
Recap
  • An array stores many values of the same type under one name
  • Array size is fixed when it's declared, and filled left to right
  • scores[i] reads the value at index i; indexing starts at 0
  • Assigning to scores[i] changes only that one element
  • A for loop repeats using a start, a condition, and a step
  • A loop's counter can be used as an array index to visit every element
  • A total variable started at 0 can add up every element in a loop
  • Valid indexes only run from 0 to size-1; going past that reads bad memory
